content.text: Open-weight models such as Llama 3.1, Mistral, Qwen 2.5, and Phi-3.5 support function calling when served through an OpenAI-compatible inference engine. Because these servers expose the same
endpoint as OpenAI, you can reuse the OpenAI Python client — just point it at a local
. No cloud dependency, no per-token billing, and full data-locality.

attrs.alt: Open model tool use stack diagram
attrs.title: Open Model Tool Use Stack

content.text: The model must be capable of tool use. Use instruct or tool-fine-tuned variants (e.g.
in Ollama). Base checkpoints without instruction tuning will not reliably emit JSON tool calls.
Start an OpenAI-compatible server locally, then point the OpenAI client at it. Ollama is the easiest starting point — install it, pull a model, and it auto-starts an HTTP server on port 11434.

# Install Ollama (macOS / Linux)
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Pull a tool-capable model
ollama pull llama3.1:8b-instruct

# The server starts automatically; verify
curl http://localhost:11434/v1/models

from openai import OpenAI

# Point client at the local Ollama server
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama"          # required by the client but not validated
)

# Verify connectivity
models = client.models.list()
for m in models.data:
    print(m.id)
Tool definitions follow the exact same OpenAI schema — a list of dicts with
and arguments arrive as a JSON string.
Complete Tool Loop with Local Model
The loop pattern is identical to OpenAI: detect
message, call the API again, repeat until
For higher throughput, vLLM provides continuous batching and serves the same OpenAI-compatible API. Start it with

# Install vLLM
pip install vllm

# Serve a tool-capable model
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
    --enable-auto-tool-choice \
    --tool-call-parser llama3_json \
    --port 8000

from openai import OpenAI
import json

# Same client, different base_url
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="EMPTY"   # vLLM ignores this
)

TOOL = [{"type": "function", "function": {
    "name": "square_root",
    "description": "Return the square root of a number.",
    "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"n": {"type": "number"}},
        "required": ["n"]
    }
}}]

import math

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the square root of 1764?"}],
    tools=TOOL
)

msg = response.choices[0].message
if msg.tool_calls:
    args = json.loads(msg.tool_calls[0].function.arguments)
    result = math.sqrt(args["n"])
    print(f"sqrt({args['n']}) = {result}")
Choosing the Right Model for Tool Use
Not all open-weight models handle tool schemas equally well. The table below shows which models reliably support function calling as of mid-2025:
Official tool-call fine-tune; parallel calls supported
Excellent JSON discipline; good schema adherence
Lightweight; may hallucinate args on complex schemas
Open models are less reliably constrained than hosted APIs and may occasionally produce malformed or incomplete argument JSON. Always parse and validate before execution.
Structured Extraction with Forced Tool Use
(or specify a named function) to force the model to always call the tool rather than produce free text. This is the standard pattern for structured extraction tasks.
Streaming works identically to OpenAI: set
chunks by index, then parse the concatenated arguments string when the stream ends.
Open-model deployments trade cloud convenience for control. Profile your setup to understand where time is spent across the tool loop.

Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, Mistral, and Hermes-2-Pro offer the strongest tool-use reliability among open models.
Always validate arguments with Pydantic before dispatch — open models are less constrained than hosted APIs.
